verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Now they which were scattered abroad upon the persecution that rose about Stephen travelled as far as Phenice, and Cyprus, and Antioch, preaching the word to none but unto the Jews only.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The persecution turned out to be no slight benefit as “to them that love God all things work together for good.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If they had made it their express study how best to establish the Church, they would have done no other thing than this—they dispersed the teachers.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark in what quarters the preaching was extended.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “They travelled,” it says, “as far as Phenice and Cyprus and Antioch; to none however did they preach the word but to Jews only.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Dost thou mark with what wise purposes of Providence so much was done in the case of Cornelius?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This serves both to justify Christ, and to impeach the Jews.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When Stephen was slain, when Paul was twice in danger, when the Apostles were scourged, then the Gentiles received the word, then the Samaritans.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Which Paul also declares: “To you it was necessary that the Word of God should first be spoken; but since ye thrust it from you, and judge yourselves unworthy, lo, we turn unto the Gentiles.” (ch. xiii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Accordingly they went about, preaching to Gentiles also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But some of them were men of Cyprus and Cyrene, who, when they were come to Antioch, spake unto the Greeks, preaching the Lord Jesus:” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 20.) for it is likely both that they could now speak Greek, and that there were such men in Antioch.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And the hand of the Lord,” it says, “was with them,” that is, they wrought miracles; “and a great number believed, and turned unto the Lord.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark why now also there was heed of miracles (namely) that they might believe?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then tidings of these things came unto the ears of the church which was in Jerusalem: and they sent forth Barnabas, that he should go as far as Antioch.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What may be the reason that, when such a city received the word, they did not come themselves?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Because of the Jews.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But they send Barnabas.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: However, it is no small part of the providential management even so that Paul comes to be there.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is both natural, and it is wisely ordered, that they are averse to him, and (so) that Voice of the Gospel, that Trumpet of heaven, is not shut up in Jerusalem.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark how on all occasions, Christ turns their ill dispositions to needful account and for the benefit of the Church?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Of their hatred to the man, He availed Himself for the building up of the Church.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But observe this holy man—Barnabas, I mean—how he looked not to his own interests, but hasted to Tarsus.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Who, when he came, and had seen the grace of God, was glad, and exhorted them all, that with purpose of heart, they would cleave unto the Lord.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he was a good man, and full of the Holy Ghost, and of faith: and much people was added unto the Lord.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He was a very kind man, and single-hearted, and considerate (συγγνωμονικός).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then departed Barnabas to Tarsus, for to seek Saul.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He came to the athletic wrestler, the general (fit to lead armies), the champion of single combat, the lion—I am at a loss for words, say what I will—the hunting-dog, killer of lions, bull of strength, lamp of brightness, mouth sufficing for a world.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when he had found him, he brought him to Antioch.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Verily this is the reason why it was there they were appointed to be called Christians, because Paul there spent so long time!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And it came to pass, that a whole year they assembled themselves with the Church, and taught much people.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the disciples were first called Christians at Antioch.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No small matter of praise to that city!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is enough to make it a match for all, that for so long a time it had the benefit of that mouth, it first, and before all others: wherefore also it was there in the first place that men were accounted worthy of that name.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you observe the benefit resulting (to that city) from Paul, to what a height that name, like a standard (σημεἵον), exalted it?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Where three thousand, where five thousand, believed, where so great a multitude, nothing of the sort took place, but they were called “they of the way:” here they were called Christians.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And in these days came prophets from Jerusalem unto Antioch.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It was need that the fruit of alms should also be planted there.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And see how of necessity (ἀναγκαίως) (it comes about that) none of the men of note becomes their teacher.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They got for their teachers, men of Cyprus, and Cyrene, and Paul—though he indeed surpassed (the Apostles) themselves—since Paul also had for teachers Ananias and Barnabas.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But here of necessity (this was the case).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And there stood up one of them named Agabus, and signified by the spirit that there would be great dearth throughout the world, which also came to pass in the days of Claudius Cæsar.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “By the Spirit,” it says: for, that they may not imagine that this was the reason why the famine came, (namely) because Christianity was come in, because the demons were departed, the Holy Ghost foretells it: this, however, was nothing wonderful, for in fact Christ predicted it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not this was the reason, else this must have been the case from the beginning: but it was because of the evils done to the Apostles—and God had borne long with them; but, when they pressed upon them, a great famine ensues, betokening to the Jews the coming woes.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “If it was because of them, in any wise it ought to have stopped (there), when it did exist.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What harm had the Gentiles done, that they should have their share in the evils?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They ought rather to have been marked as approved (εὐδοκιμἥσαι), because they were doing their part, were slaying, punishing, taking vengeance, persecuting on every side.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And mark also at what time the famine comes: precisely when the Gentiles were thenceforth added to the Church.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if, as you say, it was because of the evils (done by the Jews), these ought to have been exempted.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Christ, forestalling this objection, said, “Ye shall have tribulation.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (It is) just as if you should say, They ought not to have been scourged either.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then the disciples, every man according to his ability, determined to send relief unto the brethren which dwelt in Judea.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark how the famine becomes to them the means of salvation, an occasion of alms-giving, a harbinger of many blessing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And (so it might have been) to you, one may say, if you were so minded, but ye would not.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But it is predicted, that they might be prepared beforehand for almsgiving.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Unto the brethren which dwelt in Judæa;” for they were enduring great hardships, but before this, they were not suffering from famine.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Which also they did, and sent it to the elders by the hands of Barnabas and Saul.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark them, that no sooner do they believe than they bring forth fruit, not only for their own but for those afar off?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And Barnabas is sent and Saul, to minister (the same.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Of this occasion (᾽Ενταὕθα) he says (to the Galatians), “And James, Cephas, and John gave to me and Barnabas the right hands of fellowship, only” (they would) “that we should remember the poor.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: James was yet living.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Now they which were scattered abroad upon the persecution,” etc. (Recapitulation.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark how even in the tribulation instead of falling to lamentations and tears, as we do, they give themselves up to a great and good work?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Travelled as far as Phenice, and Cyprus, and Antioch,” and there with more security preached the word.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And some of them, which were men of Cyprus and Cyrene,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they did not say, “(What), we, Cyrenians and Cyprians, to attack this splendid and great city!” but trusting in the grace of God, they applied themselves to the work of teaching, nor did these (Gentiles) themselves think scorn to learn anything of them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark how by small means all is brought about: mark the preaching how it spreads: mark those in Jerusalem, having like care for all, holding the whole world as one house.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “They heard that Samaria had received the word, and” (ch. viii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 14) to Samaria they send the Apostles: they heard what had befallen at Antioch, and to Antioch they send Barnabas: they also send again, and (these) prophets.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the distance was great, and it was not meet the Apostles at present should separate from thence, that they might not be thought to be fugitives, and to have fled from their own people.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But then, almost precisely, is the time of their parting from Jerusalem, when the state (of the Jews) was shown to be past remedy, when the war was close at hand, and they must needs perish: when the sentence was made absolute.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, until Paul went to Rome, the Apostles were there (at Jerusalem).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But they depart, not because afraid of the war—how should it be so?—seeing those they went to, were those that should bring the war: and moreover the war breaks out only after the Apostles were dead.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For of them (the Apostles) says, “The wrath is come upon them unto the end.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (1 Thess. ii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The more insignificant the persons, the more illustrious the grace, working great results by small means.—“And he exhorted them to cleave unto the Lord, for he was a good man.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: By “good man,” I take it, he means one that is kind, (χρηστὸν) sincere, exceedingly desirous of the salvation of his neighbors—“for he was a good man, and full of the Holy Ghost and of faith.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To cleave unto the Lord with purpose of heart” (this is said): with encomium and praise.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And much people was added unto the Lord:” for like rich land this city received the word, and brought forth much fruit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then departed Barnabas to Tarsus,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But why did he take him off from Tarsus and bring him here?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not without good reason; for here were both good hopes, and a greater city, and a great body of people.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how grace works all, not Paul: by small means the affair was taking its commencement.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When it is become difficult the Apostles take it up.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why did they not before this seen Barnabas?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Because they had enough to do (ἠσχόληντο) with Jerusalem.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again they justified themselves to the Jews, that the Gentiles were receiving (προσελάμβανε) the word, even without enjoying so great attention.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There is about to be a questioning: therefore the affair of Cornelius forestalled it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then indeed they say, “That we to the Gentiles, and they to the Circumcision.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe, henceforth the very stress of the famine introduces the fellowship on the part of the Gentiles, namely, from the alms.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For they receive the offerings sent from them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Now they which were scattered abroad,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 19) and not as we who pass our time in lamentations and tears, in our calamities; but with more fearlessness they passed their time, as having got to a distance from those hindering them, and as being among men not afraid of the Jews: which also helped.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they came to Cyprus, where they had the sea between them, and greater freedom from anxiety: so they made no account of the fear of men, but (still) they gave the precedence to the regard of the Law: “they spake to Jews only.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But there were in Antioch certain men of Cyprus and Cyrene:” these, of all others, least cared for the Jews: “who spake unto the Greeks, preaching the Lord Jesus.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Probably it was because of their not knowing Hebrew, that they called them Greeks.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And “when” Barnabas, it says, “came and had seen the grace of God,”—not the diligence of men—“he exhorted them to cleave unto the Lord” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 23): and by this he converted more.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And much people was added unto the Lord.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why do they not write to Paul, but send Barnabas?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They did not yet know the virtue of the man: but it is providentially ordered that Barnabas should come.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As there was a multitude, and none to hinder, well might the faith grow, and above all because they had no trials to undergo.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Paul also preaches, and is no longer compelled to flee.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And it is well ordered, that not they speak of the famine, but the prophets.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The men of Antioch also did not take it amiss that they sent not the Apostles, but were content with their teachers: so fervent were they all for the word.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They did not wait for the famine to come, but before this they sent: “according as each had the ability.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe, among the Apostles, others are put in charge with this trust, but here Paul and Barnabas.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For this was no small order (οἰκονομία) of Providence.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Besides, it was the beginning, and it was not fit they should be offended.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “As each had the ability, they sent.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But now, none does this, although there is a famine more grievous than that.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the cases are not alike, for (all) to bear the calamity in common, and, while all (the rest) abound, for the poorer to be famishing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the expression shows that the givers also were poor, for, it says, “as each of them had the means.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: A twofold famine, even as the abundance is twofold: a severe famine, a famine not of hearing the word of the Lord, but of being nourished by alms.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then, both the poor in Judea enjoyed the benefit, and so did those in Antioch who gave their money; yea, these more than those: but now, both we and the poor are famishing: they being in lack of necessary sustenance, and we in luxurious living, lacking the mercy of God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But this is a food, than which nothing can be more necessary.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is not a food, from which one has to undergo the evils of repletion: not a food, of which the most part ends in the draught. (ἀφεδρὥνα.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nothing more beauteous, nothing more healthful, than a soul nurtured by this food: it is set high above all disease, all pestilence, all indigestion and distemper: none shall be able to overcome it, (ἑλεἵν) but just as, if one’s body were made of adamant, no iron, nor anything else, would have power to hurt it, even so when the soul is firmly compact by almsgiving, nothing at all shall be able to overcome it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For say, what shall spoil this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Shall poverty?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It cannot be, for it is laid up in the royal treasuries.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But shall robber and housebreaker?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nay, those are walls which none shall be able to break through.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But shall the worm?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nay, this treasure is set far above the reach of this mischief also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But shall envy and the evil eye?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nay, neither by these can it be overcome.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But shall false accusations and plottings of evil?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No, neither shall this be, for safe as in an asylum is this treasure.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But it were a shame should I make it appear as if the advantages which belong to almsgiving were only these (the absence of these evils), and not (the presence of) their opposites.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For in truth it is not merely that it is secure from ill-will; it also gets abundant blessing from those whom it benefits.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For as the cruel and unmerciful not only have for enemies those whom they have injured, but those also who are not themselves hurt, partake the grief and join in the accusation: so those that have done great good have not only those who are benefited, but those also who are not themselves affected, to speak their praises.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again (that), it is secure from the attacks of the evil-disposed, and robbers, and housebreakers—what, is this all the good, or is it this—that besides the not suffering diminution, it grows also and increases into multitude?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What more shameful than Nebuchadnezzar, what more foul, what more iniquitous?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The man was impious; after tokens and signs without number he refused to come to his senses (ανενεγκεἵν), but cast the servants of God into a furnace: and (yet) after these doings, he worshipped.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What then said the Prophet?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Wherefore,” saith he, “O king let my counsel be acceptable unto thee, ransom (λύτρωσαι) thy sins by alms, and thine iniquities by mercies to the poor: peradventure there shall be pardon for thy transgressions.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In so speaking, he said it not doubting, nay, with entire confidence, but wishing to put him in greater fear, and to make a stronger necessity of doing these things.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if he had spoken it as a thing unquestionable, the king would have been more supine: just as it is with us, we then most urge some person (whom we wish to persuade), when they say to us, “Exhort such an one,” and do not add, “he will be sure to hear,” but only, “peradventure he will hear:” for by leaving it doubtful, the fear is made greater, and urges him the more.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is the reason why the Prophet did not make the thing certain to him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What sayest thou?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For so great impieties shall there be pardon?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There is no sin, which alms cannot cleanse, none, which alms cannot quench: all sin is beneath this: it is a medicine adapted for every wound.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What worse than a publican?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The very matter (ὑπόθεσις) (of his occupation) is altogether one of injustice: and yet Zaccheus washed away all these (sins).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark how even Christ shows this, by the care taken to have a purse, and to bear the contributions put into it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And Paul also says, “Only that we remember the poor” (Gal. ii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 10): and everywhere the Scripture has much discourse concerning this matter.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “The ransom,” it saith, “of a man’s soul is his own wealth” (Prov. xiii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 8): and with reason: for, saith (Christ), “if thou wouldest be perfect, sell what thou hast, and give to the poor, and come, follow Me.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This may well be part of perfection.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But alms may be done not only by money, but by acts.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For example: one may kindly stand (προστἥναι) by a person (to succor and defend him), one may reach to him a helping hand: the service rendered (προστασία) by acts has often done more good even than money.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us set to work all the different kinds of almsgiving.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Can you do alms by money?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Be not slack.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Can you by good offices?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Say not, Because I have no money, this is nothing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is a very great point: look upon it as if you had given gold.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Can you do it by kind attentions (θεραπείας)?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do this also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For instance, if you be a physician, (give) your skill: for this also is a great matter.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Can you by counsel?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This (service) is much greater than all: this (alms) is better than all, or it is also more, by how much the gain it has is greater.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For in so doing you put away not starvation, but a grievous death. (ch. iii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: With such alms the Apostles above measure abounded: therefore it was that the distribution of money they put into the hands of those after them, themselves exhibiting the (mercy) shown by words.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Or is it, think you, a small alms, to a lost, castaway soul, a soul in uttermost jeopardy, possessed by a burning fever (πυρώσεως), to be able to rid it of its disease?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For example, do you see one possessed by love of money?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Pity the man.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Is he in danger of suffocation?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Quench his fire.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “What if he will not be persuaded?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do your part, and be not remiss.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Have you seen him in bonds?—for wealth is indeed bonds.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Go to him, visit him, console him, try to release him of his bonds.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If he refuse, he shall bear the blame himself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Have you seen him naked, and a stranger?—for he is indeed naked, and a stranger to heaven.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Bring him to your own inn, clothe him with the garment of virtue, give him the city which is in heaven.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “What if I myself be naked?” say you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Clothe also yourself first: if you know that you are naked, assuredly you know that you need to be clothed; if you know what sort of nakedness this is.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What numbers of women now wear silken apparel but are indeed naked of the garments of virtue!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let their husbands clothe these women.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But they will not admit those garments; they choose to have these.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then do this also first: induce them to have a longing for those garments: show them that they are naked: speak to them of judgment to come: answer me, what is the clothing we shall need there?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if ye will bear with me, I also will show you this nakedness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He that is naked, when it is cold, shrinks and shudders, and stands there cowering, and with his arms folded: but in summer heat, not so.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If then I shall prove to you that your rich men, and rich women, the more they put on, the more naked they are, do not take it amiss.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How then, I ask you, when we raise the subject of hell-fire, and of the torments there?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do not these shrink and shudder more than those naked ones?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do they not bitterly groan and condemn themselves?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What? when they come to this or that man, and say to him, Pray for me, do they not speak the same words as those (naked wretches)?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now indeed, after all that we can say, the nakedness is not yet apparent: but it will be plain enough there.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How, and in what way?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When these silken garments and precious stones shall have perished, and it shall be only by the garments of virtue and of vice that all men are shown, when the poor shall be clad with exceeding glory, but the rich, naked and in disgraceful sort, shall be haled away to their punishments.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What more naked (Edd. “more dainty”) than that rich man who arrayed himself in purple?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What poorer than Lazarus?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then which of them uttered the words of beggars? which of them was in abundance?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Say, if one should deck his house with abundance of tapestry hangings, and himself sit naked within, what were the benefit?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So it is in the case of these women.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Truly, the house of the soul, the body I mean, they hang round with plenty of garments: but the mistress of the house sits naked within.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Lend me the eyes of the soul, and I will show you the soul’s nakedness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For what is the garment of the soul?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Virtue, of course.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And what its nakedness?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For just as, if one were to strip any decent person, that person would be ashamed, and would shrink and cower out of sight; just so the soul, if we wish to see it, the soul which has not these garments, blushes for shame.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How many women, think you, at this moment feel ashamed, and would fain sink to the very depth, as if seeking some sort of curtain, or screen, that they may not hear these words?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But those who have no evil conscience, are exhilarated, rejoice, find delight, and gayly deck themselves (ἐγκαλλωπίζονται) with the things said.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hear concerning that blessed Thekla, how, that she might see Paul, she gave even her gold: and thou wilt not give even a farthing that thou mayest see Christ: thou admirest what she did, but dost not emulate her.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hearest thou not that “Blessed are the merciful, for they shall obtain mercy?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What is the gain of your costly garments? how long shall we continue agape for this attire?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us put on the glory of Christ: let us array ourselves with that beauty, that both here we may be praised, and there attain unto the eternal good things, by the grace and mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ, with Whom, to the Father and the Holy Ghost together, be glory, dominion, honor, now and ever, world without end.
